title: Treasure Hunt Engine: From Default Config to Production Ready — Veltrix Operator Perspective
author: Veltrix
excerpt: A practical breakdown of the Veltrix configuration layer that determines whether your server scales cleanly or stalls at the first growth inflection point.

content.text: Operators discover the need to tweak the Treasure Hunt Engine configuration only after they see initial deployment hiccups. The default events.yml, prizes.yml, announce.yml, and tournament.yml files are designed for small test environments and leave critical production parameters undefined. Once the server goes live, operators encounter problems such as event anomalies where the scheduled start times do not match the in‑world clock, prize allocations that misapply item rarities, and broadcast messages that are delayed or omitted. These symptoms stem from missing or hard‑coded values in the YAML files, such as default time offsets, fixed reward tiers, and insufficient error handling for invalid input. The realization that their event cadence stutters and the prize pool does
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation

Treasure Hunt Engine VX‑EVT‑01’s success on a Hytale server hinges on the meticulous layering of parameters defined in events.yml, prizes.yml, and announce.yml. At the core is the events.yml field *active* that toggles the hunt, while *start_time* and *end_time* bracket the playable window; a 2‑hour slice (e.g., 18:00–20:00) caps player commitment. Spoiler density, set by prizes.yml’s *clue_count*, directly maps to difficulty: too few clues flatten excitement, too many frustrate players. The *regen_interval* field in events.yml determines how often new items respawn; a 5‑minute interval keeps seekers engaged, whereas a 20‑minute lag allows a drop in dwell time.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

announce.yml’s *announce_interval* frequencies every 30 minutes, providing regular nudges without bombarding. The interplay of these knobs dictates the rhythm of discovery; a well‑balanced mix entices casuals to log on and veterans to replay, while a mis‑configured start_time or a high *clue_count* can produce a quick drop‑off, making the event feel stale. The reward allocation tier in prizes.yml—specifically *tier_weight*—also influences player motivation, as weighted higher‑value drops entice repeated participation.

Creating a login cadence around Treasure Hunt Engine VX‑EVT‑01 requires disciplined use of the *schedule* block in events.yml. Operators frequently falter by leaving the *repeat* flag at false, turning a daily event into a one‑off and leaving returning players without clear expectations. Setting *interval* to 1440 minutes with *start_time* at 18:00 and *end_time* at 20:00 establishes a nightly ritual. The *announce.yml* timing—*start_announce_delay* and *announce_interval*—must be in sync; a 10‑minute pre‑announcement
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

First, verify the scheduling syntax in events.yml, ensuring the 'start_time' and 'duration' are set in UTC. Next, populate prizes.yml with a mixture of drop and broadcast prizes, otherwise players will see empty reward pages. Third, configure announce.yml with chapter-wise timers; a missing 'broadcast_interval' will leave players in the dark about start times. Finally, align tournament.yml brackets to the event date; a misaligned bracket triggers duplicate match entries.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

First, prepare the event date by editing events.yml. Then (1) confirm the 'start_time' and 'end_time' fields; (2) verify prize entries in prizes.yml, ensuring each item has a valid 'name' and 'type'; (3) set up broadcast timing in announce.yml so that both leavers and joiners receive timely notice; and (4) finalize the tournament structure in tournament.yml, linking stages and matches to the event.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

The most impactful parameters in events.yml that a Veltrix operator should tune for the Treasure Hunt Engine begin with event_duration, which sets the total time a hunt runs. For a 10–50 player squad a 30‑minute slot works best; squat with 100–200 players you push it to 45 minutes, and with 500+ players extend to 60 minutes to keep the heat level manageable. next is spawn_interval, the interval between clue drop timers, and should be shortened from 120 seconds to around 90 seconds at medium scale and 60 seconds for very large rosters to prevent lag spikes. max_active_participants caps how many players can collect clues at once, which should be raised from 200 to 500 as the player base grows, otherwise game local actions collide. reward_pool_size is crucial for balancing incentives; a 10‑player event can afford a 200‑item pool, while 1000 players might need 1000 items to sustain engagement. difficulty_level adjusts the clue complexity and drop rarity; scale from “Easy” o “Hard” depending on cohort size. And finally, cooldown ensures that events do not overlap; a shorter cooldown of 20 minutes works for smaller servers, but a 30‑minute cooldown is safer when dozens of events might run in parallel.
At scale the most common break in Veltrix events is that reward_pool_size depletes too quickly, causing the engine to halt clue generation and trigger spikes in client connection timeouts.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

As an Hytale server expands, the events configuration must evolve to keep gameplay smooth. Increase spawn_interval generalization, e.g., lower from 120s to 90s or 60s to reduce the flood of network packets per tick. Simultaneously bump max_active_participants thresholds so that the server can handle more concurrent collectors without throttling. Increase reward_pool_size automatically so the system still has enough treasures to spawn at peak, perhaps scaling linearly with player count. Adjust min_spawn_distance to avoid overcrowding in the vicinity, which should grow from 15 blocks to 25 or 30 blocks for larger populations. Additionally, add a new event parameter called event_batch_limit, to run multiple treasure hunt instances in parallel without exceeding the server's object creation budget, and set a realistic value (e.g., 3 instances for 200–400 players, 5 for 500–1000). Finally, extend cooldown between event phases to give the engine time to recycle assets, reducing memory churn.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

The pre‑launch checklist for any Veltrix event requires that the events.yml file define a complete, non‑overlapping timetable of all rounds, the claims of start and finish, and that the schedule respects the Z times format used by Hytale. The prizes.yml must list each reward tier with associated points or currency, and its mapping to the event type must be correct. The announce.yml file has to include the correct broadcast intervals, the activation of the announce script, and the placeholders for event IDs and names. The tournament.yml configuration must contain a matches matrix that accurately reflects the participant list, bracket style, and result recording. Every YAML file must be free of syntax errors; a simple lint pass should return no warnings. The Treasure Hunt Engine’s event databases must be pre‑populated with valid coordinates that match the world map. The event key in events.yml must match the internal ID referenced in the engine plugin. The server must be snapshots of the world inside the event zone before the start command is issued. The default entries for each product in the events.yml must be overridden with the finalized parameters before export. All spins of the “restart‑needed” flags must have been cleared, ensuring the server can boot with the new event configuration immediately.

The pre‑launch checklist for any Veltrix event requires that the events.yml file define a complete, non‑overlapping timetable of all rounds, the claims of start and finish, and that the schedule respects the Z times format used by Hytale.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
